_doc: Module 6 quiz · 3 scenario items mapped to LO-REG.1, LO-REG.1, LO-REG.2. Every item is OPEN-JOB-AID — learner sees the §19 panel while answering. Per Rossett & Schafer (2007), the assessment validates the rep's ability to USE the aid under live conditions, not their memory of it. Per l2-quiz-blueprint.md §4.6.

items.stem: Lukas (DE hospitality, 4 locations) opens the call with: 'I don't trust new fintechs. What happens to my money if you go under?' Which §19 row is the right starting point?

options.label: KYC row · identity-verification at signup
options.rationale: KYC row anchors the trust answer (regulated KYC + smoother than bank) and chains naturally to the §13.4 bank-tier-1 segregation framing.

options.label: AML row · transaction monitoring
options.rationale: AML is about flagging suspicious transactions, not about what happens to deposited funds.

options.label: PSD2 row · open-banking framework
options.rationale: PSD2 is reference-only and doesn't speak to fund segregation.

options.label: No row · this is a product question, not regulatory
options.rationale: This is exactly the trust-anchored regulatory question §19 was written for.

items.feedback_correct: KYC opens the door. Read the §19 KYC row, then bridge to §13.4 bank-tier-1 segregation in the buyer's own words.
items.feedback_incorrect: Re-read the KYC row on the right. It's the verbatim line for the trust question — open with that, then bridge to fund segregation.

items.stem: Tom (Series-B SaaS founder) asks: 'How do you handle expense categories — does it auto-tag SaaS subscriptions vs travel?' Which §19 row do you use?

options.label: GDPR row · because it touches data
options.rationale: GDPR concerns data protection, not expense categorisation.

options.label: AML row · because monitoring sounds similar
options.rationale: AML monitors transactions for laundering risk, not for accounting categories.

options.label: No row · this is a product question, not regulatory
options.rationale: Distractor item. Do not over-apply the deflection move — answer this with Module 5 prop framing (Prop 4 · receipt capture + accounting sync).

options.label: PSD2 row · because it's EU payments
options.rationale: PSD2 doesn't touch expense categorisation; this is a workflow question.

items.feedback_correct: Right — this is a product question. Don't deflect. Frame with Prop 4 from Module 5 (receipt capture + accounting sync).
items.feedback_incorrect: This isn't a regulatory question. Over-deflecting damages your credibility. Answer with the Module 5 prop frame, not §19.

items.stem: Buyer follows up on GDPR: 'OK and what's your DSR turnaround when a Swiss ex-employee asks for their full card-transaction history under a cross-border transfer mechanism?' What do you do?

options.label: Read the GDPR row verbatim — it covers cross-border
options.rationale: The §19 GDPR row says 'data lives in the EU' and offers the DPA. It does not answer Swiss cross-border DSR timelines.

options.label: Improvise — Switzerland has an adequacy decision so it's fine
options.rationale: Don't invent. Improvising regulatory specifics on a call is exactly the failure mode §19 is designed to prevent.

options.label: Escalate to Compliance · open the Slack scaffold + book a follow-up
options.rationale: This exceeds the §19 GDPR row (specific DSR turnaround on a specific cross-border mechanism). Escalating IS the trust move per the §19 honest carve-out.

options.label: Defer the question generally — 'I'll send something later'
options.rationale: The follow-up promise without a calendar booking is the M3 anti-pattern — you'll lose the deal to follow-up drift.

items.feedback_correct: Right. Beyond §19 → escalate with a calendar invite (don't just promise email). The carve-out is the trust move.
items.feedback_incorrect: Re-read the §19 honest carve-out: when the question goes deeper than the table, escalating with a booked follow-up IS the trust move. Improvising loses the deal.

_notes.scoring: 1 point per correct item; raw score percent passes through to cmi.core.score.raw via api.complete(score).
_notes.open_job_aid_design: Per Rossett & Schafer (2007), the job-aid panel remains visible during every quiz item. The assessment tests USE of the aid under live conditions, not recall.
